title: Stacking and turning a muck heap
subtitle: Building a structured muck heap from livestock dung and soiled bedding, and turning it to produce usable compost within six months.
excerpt: Livestock dung and soiled straw bedding is an asset on a smallholding, not just a waste management problem. A correctly built and turned muck heap reaches 60 to 70 degrees C internally within the first two weeks, which kills most weed seeds and parasite eggs. After six months the material is stable, crumbly, and a valuable soil improver. After twelve months it is indistinguishable from good compost.
sourceNotes:
Environment Agency, farm waste management guidance (Farming Rules for Water). https://www.gov.uk/guidance/rules-for-farmers-and-land-managers-to-prevent-water-pollution
Natural England, farm compost guidance.

content.text: Site the muck heap on firm, dry ground at least 10 metres from any watercourse, ditch, or land drain, and downhill from any water supply. A concrete base is ideal because it prevents leachate running off into the ground. If no concrete base is available, compact the ground and use a sacrificial layer of straw or old hay at the base to absorb liquid.

Add material in layers as it comes from mucking out. Aim for a mix of roughly two parts straw bedding to one part dung by volume: a heap that is too wet (mostly dung with little straw) becomes anaerobic and slimy; one that is too dry (mostly clean straw) does not heat up. When the heap reaches about 1.5 metres high, cap it with a layer of straw or old hay to insulate it and start the
. Begin a new heap beside it rather than adding more to the capped one.

Turn the heap once at four to six weeks: the outside material goes into the centre and the centre material goes to the outside. This re-inoculates the cooler outer layers with active microbes and ensures uniform composting. After turning, the heap should reheat within two to three days. Turn a second time at three months. By six months the material should be dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling with no recognisable straw.

runs from 1 October to 31 January for most land in England. Well-composted manure after six months loses much of its fast-release nitrogen compared with fresh muck, which makes it a more stable and less water-pollution-risk soil improver. Pig muck should be composted for at least twelve months before spreading on any land that may be used for food crops, as a precaution against pathogen survival.
